title: Data Mastery
tagline: Work with lists, objects, and real data.

accomplishments: Create a list of favorite foods and display them
Add new items to a list with a button click
Build a dynamic to-do list that grows as you add tasks

do.title: Display a List of Items
steps: Click "Show My List" — see all 5 foods appear!
do.code: My Favorite Foods
do.result: You stored 5 items in ONE variable (an array), then displayed them all!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing all 5 foods displayed in a numbered list.

concepts.name: What is an array?
concepts.definition: A list of items stored in ONE variable. Created with square brackets []. Items are separated by commas.
concepts.analogy: A numbered row of boxes. Each box holds one item, and you access them by their position number.

concepts.name: Index numbers start at 0
concepts.definition: The first item is [0], second is [1], third is [2]. This is universal across programming languages.
concepts.analogy: Floor numbers in many buildings: ground floor = 0, first floor = 1.

concepts.name: .push() adds items
concepts.definition: arrayName.push("new item") adds an item to the END of the array. The array automatically grows.
concepts.analogy: Adding a new box to the end of your row of boxes.

concepts.name: .length counts items
concepts.definition: arrayName.length returns how many items are in the array. Updates automatically when you add or remove items.
concepts.analogy: Counting how many boxes are in the row.

concepts.name: for loops repeat for each item
concepts.definition: for (let i = 0; i < array.length; i++) { } repeats code once for every item in the array.
concepts.analogy: Going through each box one by one, doing the same thing to each.

understand.keyTakeaway: Arrays store multiple related items together. Access by index [0], add with .push(), count with .length.

build.goal: Replace foods with YOUR favorites and add a 6th with .push()
build.hint: .push() adds to the END. The array automatically expands!

challenge.title: Build a Dynamic To-Do List
challenge.mission: Create a to-do list where users type tasks and add them with a button.
requirements: Display all tasks in a numbered list
Tasks persist when you add new ones
challenge.starterCode: ✅ My To-Do List
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a "Clear All Tasks" button that empties the array!

capabilities: Create arrays to store multiple items
Loop through arrays with for loops
Build dynamic lists from user input

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn how to REMOVE items, FILTER lists, and TRANSFORM data using powerful array methods.

accomplishments: Remove items from a list with one click
Filter a list to show only certain items
Transform an entire list with one line of code

do.title: Build a Shopping Cart with Remove
do.code: 🛒 Shopping Cart
do.result: .pop() removed the last item from the array. The array got smaller!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the cart after adding 2 apples, then removing 1.

concepts.name: .pop() removes the last item
concepts.definition: Removes AND returns the last item from the array. The array shrinks by one.
concepts.analogy: Taking the top book off a stack.

concepts.name: .shift() removes the first item
concepts.definition: Removes AND returns the first item. Everything else shifts down one position.
concepts.analogy: People leaving the front of a line.

concepts.name: .filter() creates a new filtered array
concepts.definition: array.filter(function(item) { return condition; }) returns a NEW array with only items that pass the test.
concepts.analogy: A sieve — pour everything through, only what fits the holes comes out.

concepts.name: The % modulo operator
concepts.definition: Returns the remainder after division. 8 % 2 === 0 (even). 7 % 2 === 1 (odd). Used to check divisibility.
concepts.analogy: What's left over after dividing evenly — like splitting cookies among friends.

concepts.name: .filter() doesn't change the original
concepts.definition: It creates a BRAND NEW array. The original stays untouched. This is called a non-destructive method.
concepts.analogy: Photocopying only certain pages — the original book is unchanged.

concepts.name: push/pop vs unshift/shift
concepts.definition: push/pop work on the END. unshift/shift work on the START. Push adds, pop removes. Unshift adds, shift removes.
concepts.analogy: Two doors on a bus — front door (shift/unshift) and back door (push/pop).

understand.keyTakeaway: .pop() removes from end, .shift() removes from start, .filter() creates a new array with only matching items.

build.goal: Filter a task list to show only incomplete tasks
build.hint: .filter() doesn't change the original array. It creates a NEW one!

challenge.title: Build a Number Filter
challenge.mission: Create a number list with buttons to filter: All, Even, Odd, Greater Than 10.
challenge.starterCode: <!-- Create number-filter.html with filter buttons and display area -->

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn about OBJECTS — how to organize related data together (like a person's name, age, and email in one place).

accomplishments: Create a person object with name, age, and hobbies
Make a mini database of multiple people

do.title: Create Your First Object
steps: Click "Show Person" to see the info!
do.code:
Person Information

Click the button to show info
do.result: Instead of 4 separate variables, you stored all related information in ONE object!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the person card with all 4 pieces of information.

concepts.name: What is an object?
concepts.definition: A collection of related properties (key-value pairs) stored together in curly braces {}.
concepts.analogy: A digital file folder with labeled sections — each section has a name and content.

concepts.name: Properties are key-value pairs
concepts.definition: Each property has a name (key) and a value, separated by a colon. Properties are separated by commas.
concepts.analogy: A label on a box: the label is the key, what's inside is the value.

concepts.name: Dot notation
concepts.definition: Access properties with objectName.propertyName. Example: person.name returns "Sarah".
concepts.analogy: Opening a folder (person) and finding a specific document (name).

concepts.name: Objects vs Arrays
concepts.definition: Arrays = lists of similar items (accessed by position). Objects = related properties about one thing (accessed by name).
concepts.analogy: Array = a shopping list. Object = a product's details (name, price, color).

concepts.name: Objects can hold arrays
concepts.definition: Properties can be any data type including arrays: skills: ["HTML", "CSS", "JS"]
concepts.analogy: A folder can contain lists — like a person folder containing a list of hobbies.

concepts.name: Arrays of objects = databases
concepts.definition: An array where each item is an object: [{name: "A"}, {name: "B"}]. This is how real apps store data.
concepts.analogy: A spreadsheet — each row is an object, each column is a property.

understand.keyTakeaway: Objects group related data together. If information describes the same thing, it belongs in an object.

build.goal: Create YOUR digital business card with an object
build.hint: Make sure each property is separated by a comma. The last property doesn't need one.

challenge.title: Build a Contact Database
challenge.mission: Create an array of 4+ contact objects and display them as cards in a grid.
requirements: Array with at least 4 contact objects
Each has: name, phone, email, relationship
Show all contact info on each card
challenge.starterCode: <!-- Create contact-database.html with a contacts array and displayContacts() function -->
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add filter buttons: "Show All", "Friends Only", "Family Only", "Work Only"!

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll combine everything — arrays, objects, loops, and filters — to build a complete student grade dashboard.

accomplishments: Build a student grade tracker that calculates averages

do.title: Calculate Grade Statistics
steps: Click "Calculate Statistics" to see the results!
do.code:
📊 Grade Statistics

HIGHEST

-

LOWEST

-

AVERAGE

-
do.result: You analyzed an array of numbers and calculated statistics — just like real data analysis!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the three statistics calculated.

concepts.name: Finding max/min with loops
concepts.definition: Start with the first item as the current max. Loop through the rest — if any item is bigger, it becomes the new max. Same logic reversed for min.
concepts.analogy: Going through a stack of papers, keeping track of the highest number you've seen.

concepts.name: Calculating averages
concepts.definition: Sum all values in a loop, then divide by the count (array.length). average = sum / array.length.
concepts.analogy: Adding up everyone's age at a party, then dividing by number of people.

concepts.name: .toFixed() for rounding
concepts.definition: number.toFixed(1) rounds to 1 decimal place. .toFixed(2) rounds to 2. Returns a string.
concepts.analogy: Rounding to the nearest penny — you choose how precise.

concepts.name: Combining filter + display
concepts.definition: Filter an array, then pass the filtered result to a display function. The original data stays unchanged.
concepts.analogy: Sorting mail into piles (filter), then reading one pile at a time (display).

concepts.name: Everything working together
concepts.definition: Real apps combine arrays (data), objects (structure), loops (processing), filters (selection), and DOM updates (display).
concepts.analogy: An orchestra — each instrument plays its part, together they make music.

understand.keyTakeaway: Statistics are just loops with comparisons. Combine arrays, objects, loops, and filters to build real data applications.

build.goal: Build a complete student dashboard with filtering
build.hint: Use .filter() to create passing and failing arrays, then display each set with its own function call.

challenge.title: Complete Student Grade Dashboard
challenge.mission: Build a full dashboard with student cards, statistics, and pass/fail filtering.
requirements: Stats: total, passing count, failing count, average
Each card shows pass/fail status badge

bridge.preview: Module 2 complete! You've built to-do lists, shopping carts, contact databases, and grade dashboards. In Module 3, you'll master user interaction — forms, validation, keyboard events, and real user experiences.
